19.o: Who can behold the holy Jesus, looking forward to the miseries that awaited his murderers, weeping over the city where his precious blood was about to be shed, without seeing that the likeness of God in the believer, consists much in good-will and compassion? Surely those cannot be right who take up any doctrines of truth, so as to be hardened towards their fellow-sinners. But let every one remember, that though Jesus wept over Jerusalem, he executed awful vengeance upon it. Though he delights not in the death of a sinner, yet he will surely bring to pass his awful threatenings on those who neglect his salvation. The Son of God did not weep vain and causeless tears, nor for a light matter, nor for himself. He knows the value of souls, the weight of guilt, and how low it will press and sink mankind. May he then come and cleanse our hearts by his Spirit, from all that defiles. May sinners, on every side, become attentive to the words of truth and salvation.
19.m:
Who can look at the holy Jesus, crying because of the suffering waiting for those who would kill him, and not see that true believers show kindness and care? Those who hold onto religious teachings should not become hard-hearted toward others. 

But everyone should remember that even though Jesus cried for Jerusalem, he still punished it severely later. Although he doesn’t enjoy seeing people die, he will carry out his warnings against those who ignore his offer of salvation. 

Jesus didn't cry useless or empty tears. He knew the worth of each soul and understood how deeply guilty actions hurt people. Let us ask God to clean our hearts through his Spirit, removing anything impure. And may sinners everywhere listen carefully to messages of truth and salvation.

20.o: Men often pretend to examine the evidences of revelation, and the truth of the gospel, when only seeking excuses for their own unbelief and disobedience. Christ answered these priests and scribes with a plain question about the baptism of John, which the common people could answer. They all knew it was from heaven, nothing in it had an earthly tendency. Those that bury the knowledge they have, are justly denied further knowledge. It was just with Christ to refuse to give account of his authority, to those who knew the baptism of John to be from heaven, yet would not believe in him, nor own their knowledge.
20.m: People often claim they want to check if what God says is true, but really they're looking for reasons to doubt and disobey. Jesus asked some priests and teachers a simple question about John’s baptism. Everyone knew it came from God, not anything worldly. People who hide what they know lose out on learning more. Jesus didn’t feel he needed to explain himself to those who refused to accept both John’s message and his own teachings, even though they knew John’s baptism was from God.

20.o: Christ spake this parable against those who resolved not to own his authority, though the evidence of it was so full. How many resemble the Jews who murdered the prophets and crucified Christ, in their enmity to God, and aversion to his service, desiring to live according to their lusts, without control! Let all who are favoured with God's word, look to it that they make proper use of their advantages. Awful will be the doom, both of those who reject the Son, and of those who profess to reverence Him, yet render not the fruits in due season. Though they could not but own that for such a sin, such a punishment was just, yet they could not bear to hear of it. It is the folly of sinners, that they persevere in sinful ways, though they dread the destruction at the end of those ways.
20.m:
Jesus told this story about people who refused to accept his authority even though there was plenty of proof. Many people today act like the ones who killed the prophets and Jesus—they hate God and don't want to follow him. They want to do whatever they please without limits.

Everyone who has access to God’s word should use it properly. Those who reject Jesus and those who claim to respect him but don’t show it by living right will face terrible judgment. Even if they agree that such punishment fits the crime, they still don’t like hearing about it.

It’s foolish for sinners to keep doing wrong things because they’re afraid of the punishment at the end.

20.o: Those who are most crafty in their designs against Christ and his gospel, cannot hide them. He did not give a direct answer, but reproved them for offering to impose upon him; and they could not fasten upon any thing wherewith to stir up either the governor or the people against him. The wisdom which is from above, will direct all who teach the way of God truly, to avoid the snares laid for them by wicked men; and will teach our duty to God, to our rulers, and to all men, so clearly, that opposers will have no evil to say of us.
20.m:
Those who try to trick Jesus and his teachings won't succeed. He didn’t answer directly but told them he wasn’t going to let them fool him. They couldn’t find anything to use against him to upset the ruler or the people.

The smart people who teach about God’s truth know how to avoid traps set by bad people. They learn how to do what is right toward God, leaders, and everyone else. This makes it hard for those who oppose them to say anything bad about them.

20.o: It is common for those who design to undermine any truth of God, to load it with difficulties. But we wrong ourselves, and wrong the truth of Christ, when we form our notions of the world of spirits by this world of sense. There are more worlds than one; a present visible world, and a future unseen world; and let every one compare this world and that world, and give the preference in his thoughts and cares to that which deserves them. Believers shall obtain the resurrection from the dead, that is the blessed resurrection. What shall be the happy state of the inhabitants of that world, we cannot express or conceive, 1Co 2:9. Those that are entered into the joy of their Lord, are entirely taken up therewith; when there is perfection of holiness there will be no occasion for preservatives from sin. And when God called himself the God of these patriarchs, he meant that he was a God all-sufficient to them, Ge 17:1, their exceeding great Reward, Ge 15:1. He never did that for them in this world, which answered the full extent of his undertaking; therefore there must be another life, in which he will do that for them, which will completely fulfil the promise.
20.m:
People often try to confuse others about God’s truths by making them seem hard to understand. We hurt ourselves and the teachings of Jesus when we think only about what we see around us. 

There are different kinds of worlds. One is the world we can see now, and another is the world we can’t see yet. Think carefully about both worlds and focus on the one that really matters.

Believers get the good resurrection after death. We can't fully describe what it will be like, but it will be wonderful—far better than anything here. People who enter heaven will be so happy there that nothing else will matter. When someone is perfect, they won’t want to sin anymore.

God calls himself the all-powerful God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. That means he gives them everything they need. In this life, God hasn’t given them everything promised. So, there must be another life where he fulfills all his promises.

20.o: The scribes commended the reply Christ made to the Sadducees about the resurrection, but they were silenced by a question concerning the Messiah. Christ, as God, was David's Lord; but Christ, as man, was David's son. The scribes would receive the severest judgement for defrauding the poor widows, and for their abuse of religion, particularly of prayer, which they used as a pretence for carrying on worldly and wicked plans. Dissembled piety is double sin. Then let us beg of God to keep us from pride, ambition, covetousness, and every evil thing; and to teach us to seek that honour which comes from him alone.
20.m:
The scribes liked how Jesus answered the Sadducees about the resurrection. But they couldn't answer his question about the Messiah. 

Jesus said he was both God and David’s Lord. He also said he was human and David’s son. 

The scribes will face serious judgment for cheating poor widows and using religion wrongly, especially prayers, to do selfish things. 

Fake piety makes you doubly guilty. 

So let’s ask God to protect us from pride, ambition, greed, and all bad things. And help us seek honor only from God.

21.o: From the offering of this poor widow, learn that what we rightly give for the relief of the poor, and the support of God's worship, is given unto God; and our Saviour sees with pleasure whatever we have in our hearts to give for the relief of his members, or for his service. Blessed Lord! the poorest of thy servants have two mites, they have a soul and a body; persuade and enable us to offer both unto thee; how happy shall we be in thine accepting of them!
21.m: From the offering of this poor widow, we learn that anything we give to help the needy or support God’s work is given to God. Jesus sees with joy whatever we put into helping others or serving him. Dear Lord, even the poorest of your people have something small to give—like two coins—or their very selves. Help us to offer these gifts to you. How blessed we will be if you accept them!

21.o: With much curiosity those about Christ ask as to the time when the great desolation should be. He answers with clearness and fulness, as far as was necessary to teach them their duty; for all knowledge is desirable as far as it is in order to practice. Though spiritual judgements are the most common in gospel times, yet God makes use of temporal judgments also. Christ tells them what hard things they should suffer for his name's sake, and encourages them to bear up under their trials, and to go on in their work, notwithstanding the opposition they would meet with. God will stand by you, and own you, and assist you. This was remarkably fulfilled after the pouring out of the Spirit, by whom Christ gave his disciples wisdom and utterance. Though we may be losers for Christ, we shall not, we cannot be losers by him, in the end. It is our duty and interest at all times, especially in perilous, trying times, to secure the safety of our own souls. It is by Christian patience we keep possession of our own souls, and keep out all those impressions which would put us out of temper. We may view the prophecy before us much as those Old Testament prophecies, which, together with their great object, embrace, or glance at some nearer object of importance to the church. Having given an idea of the times for about thirty-eight years next to come, Christ shows what all those things would end in, namely, the destruction of Jerusalem, and the utter dispersion of the Jewish nation; which would be a type and figure of Christ's second coming. The scattered Jews around us preach the truth of Christianity; and prove, that though heaven and earth shall pass away, the words of Jesus shall not pass away. They also remind us to pray for those times when neither the real, nor the spiritual Jerusalem, shall any longer be trodden down by the Gentiles, and when both Jews and Gentiles shall be turned to the Lord. When Christ came to destroy the Jews, he came to redeem the Christians that were persecuted and oppressed by them; and then had the churches rest. When he comes to judge the world, he will redeem all that are his from their troubles. So fully did the Divine judgements come upon the Jews, that their city is set as an example before us, to show that sins will not pass unpunished; and that the terrors of the Lord, and his threatenings against impenitent sinners, will all come to pass, even as his word was true, and his wrath great upon Jerusalem.
21.m:
People asked Jesus when the big trouble would start. He answered clearly enough to help them know what to do. Even though bad things often happen spiritually, God sometimes uses physical problems too. 

Jesus told them they would face many challenges because of him. But he encouraged them to stay strong and keep doing their work, even if people tried to stop them. God will support you and help you through tough times. 

This promise was seen after the Holy Spirit came and gave the followers wisdom and courage. Even if we lose things for Jesus, we won’t really lose anything important in the end. In dangerous times, it’s best to focus on saving your soul. Patience helps you hold onto your faith and avoid getting upset.

We can see this prophecy like some old prophecies that talk about future events while mentioning closer ones too. After giving hints about what would happen over the next 38 years, Jesus said these things would lead to the destruction of Jerusalem and scattering of the Jewish people. This showed what would happen when he returned.

The spread of Jewish people today still proves that Jesus’ words are true. Heaven and Earth might end, but Jesus' promises won't. Their situation reminds us to pray for the day when both the real and spiritual Jerusalem will be free from oppression. Then, both Jews and Gentiles will turn to God.

When Jesus came to punish the Jews, he saved the Christians who suffered under them. And the churches found peace. When he returns to judge the world, he will rescue all his followers from their troubles.

The punishments from God came so completely on the Jews that their city serves as an example for us. It shows that sins won't go unnoticed. The fearsome consequences that God warned about for those who don’t repent will surely happen. Just as God’s word promised, his anger fell heavily on Jerusalem.

21.o: Christ tells his disciples to observe the signs of the times, which they might judge by. He charges them to look upon the ruin of the Jewish nation as near. Yet this race and family of Abraham shall not be rooted out; it shall survive as a nation, and be found as prophesied, when the Son of man shall be revealed. He cautions them against being secure and sensual. This command is given to all Christ's disciples, Take heed to yourselves, that ye be not overpowered by temptations, nor betrayed by your own corruptions. We cannot be safe, if we are carnally secure. Our danger is, lest the day of death and of judgment should come upon us when we are not prepared. Lest, when we are called to meet our Lord, that be the furthest from our thoughts, which ought to be nearest our hearts. For so it will come upon the most of men, who dwell upon the earth, and mind earthly things only, and have no converse with heaven. It will be a terror and a destruction to them. Here see what should be our aim, that we may be accounted worthy to escape all those things; that when the judgements of God are abroad, we may not be in the common calamity, or it may not be that to us which it is to others. Do you ask how you may be found worthy to stand before Christ at that day? Those who never yet sought Christ, let them now go unto him; those who never yet were humbled for their sins, let them now begin; those who have already begun, let them go forward and be kept humbled. Watch therefore, and pray always. Watch against sin; watch in every duty, and make the most of every opportunity to do good. Pray always: those shall be accounted worthy to live a life of praise in the other world, who live a life of prayer in this world. May we begin, employ, and conclude each day attending to Christ's word, obeying his precepts, and following his example, that whenever he comes we may be found watching.
21.m:
Jesus told his followers to pay attention to the signs of what was coming. He said the end of the Jewish nation was near, but some people would still survive. 

He warned them not to become too relaxed or focused on worldly pleasures. He gave this warning to everyone who follows him: Be careful not to get caught off guard by temptation or your own weaknesses. You won’t be safe if you’re too comfortable. The day could come suddenly when you least expect it—like the day of death or final judgment. Don't let that day surprise you. Most people think only about earthly things and don't consider heavenly matters. That will bring fear and trouble to them.

To avoid such troubles, Jesus said we need to stay alert and humble ourselves. Ask yourself how you can be ready for the future. If you haven’t started seeking Christ, start now. If you haven’t repented of your sins, repent now. And if you’ve already started, keep going. Stay alert and pray constantly. Guard against sinning, and use every chance to do good. Always pray, because those who pray here will receive praise in the next world.

Let’s begin each day by listening to Christ’s words, following his commands, and copying his actions. Then we’ll be ready when he returns.

22.o: Christ knew all men, and had wise and holy ends in taking Judas to be a disciple. How he who knew Christ so well, came to betray him, we are here told; Satan entered into Judas. It is hard to say whether more mischief is done to Christ's kingdom, by the power of its open enemies, or by the treachery of its pretended friends; but without the latter, its enemies could not do so much evil as they do.
22.m:
Jesus knew everything about everyone, and he chose Judas as one of his disciples for a reason. We're told here how someone who knew Jesus so well ended up betraying him—Satan took control of Judas. 

It's difficult to say if more harm comes from the open enemies of Christ's kingdom or from those who pretend to be his friends but act against him. But without these traitors, the open enemies couldn't cause as much damage.

22.o: Christ kept the ordinances of the law, particularly that of the passover, to teach us to observe his gospel institutions, and most of all that of the Lord's supper. Those who go upon Christ's word, need not fear disappointment. According to the orders given them, the disciples got all ready for the passover. Jesus bids this passover welcome. He desired it, though he knew his sufferings would follow, because it was in order to his Father's glory and man's redemption. He takes his leave of all passovers, signifying thereby his doing away all the ordinances of the ceremonial law, of which the passover was one of the earliest and chief. That type was laid aside, because now in the kingdom of God the substance was come.
22.m:
Christ followed the rules of the law, especially the Passover, to show us how to keep the new rules of his church, and most importantly, the Lord’s Supper. People who trust what Christ says don’t have to worry about being let down. The disciples prepared everything for the Passover just as they were told. 

Jesus welcomed the Passover even though he knew suffering would follow, because it honored his Father and helped save people. This Passover was special because Jesus was ending all the old rules of the ceremonial law, including the Passover. These types were done away with because now the real thing had arrived in God’s kingdom.

22.o: The Lord's supper is a sign or memorial of Christ already come, who by dying delivered us; his death is in special manner set before us in that ordinance, by which we are reminded of it. The breaking of Christ's body as a sacrifice for us, is therein brought to our remembrance by the breaking of bread. Nothing can be more nourishing and satisfying to the soul, than the doctrine of Christ's making atonement for sin, and the assurance of an interest in that atonement. Therefore we do this in rememberance of what He did for us, when he died for us; and for a memorial of what we do, in joining ourselves to him in an everlasting covenant. The shedding of Christ's blood, by which the atonement was made, is represented by the wine in the cup.
22.m: The Lord's Supper reminds us that Jesus has already come and died to save us. When we take Communion, we think about how he gave himself up for us. Breaking the bread helps us remember how Jesus sacrificed his body for us. There is nothing better for our souls than knowing that Jesus paid for our sins and that we share in that payment. So, we do this to remember what Jesus did when he died for us. We also join together in a permanent agreement with him. The wine in the cup represents the blood Jesus shed to make the payment for our sins.

22.o: How unbecoming is the worldly ambition of being the greatest, to the character of a follower of Jesus, who took upon him the form of a servant, and humbled himself to the death of the cross! In the way to eternal happiness, we must expect to be assaulted and sifted by Satan. If he cannot destroy, he will try to disgrace or distress us. Nothing more certainly forebodes a fall, in a professed follower of Christ, than self-confidence, with disregard to warnings, and contempt of danger. Unless we watch and pray always, we may be drawn in the course of the day into those sins which we were in the morning most resolved against. If believers were left to themselves, they would fall; but they are kept by the power of God, and the prayer of Christ. Our Lord gave notice of a very great change of circumstances now approaching. The disciples must not expect that their friends would be kind to them as they had been. Therefore, he that has a purse, let him take it, for he may need it. They must now expect that their enemies would be more fierce than they had been, and they would need weapons. At the time the apostles understood Christ to mean real weapons, but he spake only of the weapons of the spiritual warfare. The sword of the Spirit is the sword with which the disciples of Christ must furnish themselves.
22.m:
It’s wrong for followers of Jesus to want to be the most important person. Jesus became humble and died on a cross. On our path to heaven, we face attacks from Satan. He tries to shame or hurt us if he can’t destroy us. A believer in Christ falls when they become too confident and ignore warnings or dangers. We might do things we promised ourselves we wouldn't do unless we stay alert and pray all the time. Believers would fail without God’s help and Christ’s prayers. 

Jesus told his followers about big changes coming soon. Friends might stop being nice to them. So anyone who has money should keep it because they’ll need it. Enemies could get angrier, so they might need tools. But at first, the disciples thought Jesus meant actual weapons. Really, he was talking about spiritual weapons. Followers of Christ need the sword of the Spirit.

22.o: Every description which the evangelists give of the state of mind in which our Lord entered upon this conflict, proves the tremendous nature of the assault, and the perfect foreknowledge of its terrors possessed by the meek and lowly Jesus. Here are three things not in the other evangelists. 1. When Christ was in his agony, there appeared to him an angel from heaven, strengthening him. It was a part of his humiliation that he was thus strengthened by a ministering spirit. 2. Being in agony, he prayed more earnestly. Prayer, though never out of season, is in a special manner seasonable when we are in an agony. 3. In this agony his sweat was as it were great drops of blood falling down. This showed the travail of his soul. We should pray also to be enabled to resist unto the shedding of our blood, striving against sin, if ever called to it. When next you dwell in imagination upon the delights of some favourite sin, think of its effects as you behold them here! See its fearful effects in the garden of Gethsemane, and desire, by the help of God, deeply to hate and to forsake that enemy, to ransom sinners from whom the Redeemer prayed, agonized, and bled.
22.m:
Every description given by the writers shows how terrible the struggle was for Jesus and how much he knew about what would happen. There are three details here that the others don’t mention:

1. An angel came to strengthen Jesus when he was in deep pain.
   - This was part of Jesus' suffering because he needed help from a heavenly being.

2. Jesus prayed very hard while he was in pain.
   - Praying is always good, but especially helpful during times of great stress.

3. His sweat looked like big drops of blood.
   - This showed how much Jesus was hurting inside.

We should ask God to help us stand strong even if it means suffering too. 

Next time you imagine enjoying a favorite sin, remember how it hurt Jesus so badly in the garden. Think about the scary results of sin as you see them here. Ask God to help you really hate and stop those sins, just like Jesus did when he asked God to save people from their sins.

22.o: Nothing can be a greater affront or grief to the Lord Jesus, than to be betrayed by those who profess to be his followers, and say that they love him. Many instances there are, of Christ's being betrayed by those who, under the form of godliness, fight against the power of it. Jesus here gave an illustrious example of his own rule of doing good to those that hate us, as afterwards he did of praying for those that despitefully use us. Corrupt nature warps our conduct to extremes; we should seek for the Lord's direction before we act in difficult circumstances. Christ was willing to wait for his triumphs till his warfare was accomplished, and we must be so too. But the hour and the power of darkness were short, and such the triumphs of the wicked always will be.
22.m: Nothing upsets the Lord Jesus more than being betrayed by people who claim to follow him and say they love him. There have been many times when Christ has been betrayed by people who pretend to be religious but actually work against true goodness. Here, Jesus sets a great example by showing kindness to those who hate him, just as he later showed by praying for those who mistreat him. Our natural tendencies often lead us to extreme actions, so we need God’s guidance before acting in tough situations. Like Jesus, we should wait patiently for our victories until all our tasks are done. However, the time and power of evil are limited, and the victories of bad people never last long.

22.o: Peter's fall was his denying that he knew Christ, and was his disciple; disowning him because of distress and danger. He that has once told a lie, is strongly tempted to persist: the beginning of that sin, like strife, is as the letting forth of water. The Lord turned and looked upon Peter. 1. It was a convincing look. Jesus turned and looked upon him, as if he should say, Dost thou not know me, Peter? 2. It was a chiding look. Let us think with what a rebuking countenance Christ may justly look upon us when we have sinned. 3. It was an expostulating look. Thou who wast the most forward to confess me to be the Son of God, and didst solemnly promise thou wouldest never disown me! 4. It was a compassionate look. Peter, how art thou fallen and undone if I do not help thee! 5. It was a directing look, to go and bethink himself. 6. It was a significant look; it signified the conveying of grace to Peter's heart, to enable him to repent. The grace of God works in and by the word of God, brings that to mind, and sets that home upon the conscience, and so gives the soul the happy turn. Christ looked upon the chief priests, and made no impression upon them as he did on Peter. It was not the mere look from Christ, but the Divine grace with it, that restored Peter.
22.m:
Peter denied knowing Jesus and being one of his followers out of fear and danger. Once someone tells a lie, they often keep lying. Lying starts like opening a floodgate.

Jesus turned and looked at Peter. 

First, it was a convincing look, almost saying, "Do you really not know me, Peter?"

Second, it was a chiding look, showing how Jesus could rightly be upset with people who sin.

Third, it was an asking look, wondering how Peter, who had boldly said Jesus was the Son of God, could now deny him.

Fourth, it was a caring look, showing concern for Peter’s well-being.

Fifth, it was a guiding look, urging Peter to reflect on his actions.

Sixth, it was a meaningful look, bringing grace to Peter’s heart to help him feel sorry for what he did.

God’s grace works through the Word of God, reminding us of our promises and making us feel guilty enough to want to change.

When Jesus looked at the high priests, he didn’t affect them like he did Peter. It wasn't just Jesus’ look alone, but God’s grace working together with it that helped restore Peter.

22.o: Those that condemned Jesus for a blasphemer, were the vilest blasphemers. He referred them to his second coming, for the full proof of his being the Christ, to their confusion, since they would not admit the proof of it to their conviction. He owns himself to be the Son of God, though he knew he should suffer for it. Upon this they ground his condemnation. Their eyes being blinded, they rush on. Let us meditate on this amazing transaction, and consider Him who endured such contradiction of sinners against himself.
22.m:
Those who called Jesus a blasphemer were actually the worst kind of blasphemers themselves. He told them that at his return, everyone would see that he was the Messiah, which would prove embarrassing for them because they refused to believe it now. 

Jesus admitted he was the Son of God, even though he knew it would lead to his suffering. They used this admission as reason to condemn him. Because they were blind to the truth, they kept attacking him. We should think about this incredible event and remember the one who faced so much opposition from sinners.

23.o: Pilate well understood the difference between armed forces and our Lord's followers. But instead of being softened by Pilate's declaration of his innocence, and considering whether they were not bringing the guilt of innocent blood upon themselves, the Jews were the more angry. The Lord brings his designs to a glorious end, even by means of those who follow the devices of their own hearts. Thus all parties joined, so as to prove the innocence of Jesus, who was the atoning sacrifice for our sins.
23.m:
Pilate clearly saw the difference between soldiers and Jesus' followers. Instead of feeling better about Jesus after Pilate said he was innocent, the Jews got angrier. 

The Lord still brought good things from what people did out of selfish reasons. Everyone involved helped show that Jesus was innocent. He died as the sacrifice for our sins.

23.o: Herod had heard many things of Jesus in Galilee, and out of curiosity longed to see him. The poorest beggar that asked a miracle for the relief of his necessity, was never denied; but this proud prince, who asked for a miracle only to gratify his curiosity, is refused. He might have seen Christ and his wondrous works in Galilee, and would not, therefore it is justly said, Now he would see them, and shall not. Herod sent Christ again to Pilate: the friendships of wicked men are often formed by union in wickedness. They agree in little, except in enmity to God, and contempt of Christ.
23.m:
Herod had heard about Jesus in Galilee and wanted to see him out of curiosity. Even the poorest beggar who asked for a miracle got what they needed, but Herod, who only wanted a miracle to satisfy his curiosity, was turned down. He could have seen Jesus and his amazing works in Galilee if he really wanted to, but now he won't get the chance. 

Herod sent Jesus back to Pilate. Wicked people often become friends because they share evil plans. They don’t agree on much, except being against God and looking down on Christ.

23.o: The fear of man brings many into this snare, that they will do an unjust thing, against their consciences, rather than get into trouble. Pilate declares Jesus innocent, and has a mind to release him; yet, to please the people, he would punish him as an evil-doer. If no fault be found in him, why chastise him? Pilate yielded at length; he had not courage to go against so strong a stream. He delivered Jesus to their will, to be crucified.
23.m:
Many people do wrong things out of fear of others. They might do something unfair just to avoid getting in trouble. 

Pilate said Jesus was innocent and wanted to let him go free. But to please the crowd, he decided to punish Jesus anyway. 

If there’s no proof that Jesus did anything bad, why should he be beaten? 

In the end, Pilate gave in. He didn’t have the courage to stand up to them. So he handed Jesus over to be crucified, even though he knew Jesus was innocent.

23.o: We have here the blessed Jesus, the Lamb of God, led as a lamb to the slaughter, to the sacrifice. Though many reproached and reviled him, yet some pitied him. But the death of Christ was his victory and triumph over his enemies: it was our deliverance, the purchase of eternal life for us. Therefore weep not for him, but let us weep for our own sins, and the sins of our children, which caused his death; and weep for fear of the miseries we shall bring upon ourselves, if we slight his love, and reject his grace. If God delivered him up to such sufferings as these, because he was made a sacrifice for sin, what will he do with sinners themselves, who make themselves a dry tree, a corrupt and wicked generation, and good for nothing! The bitter sufferings of our Lord Jesus should make us stand in awe of the justice of God. The best saints, compared with Christ, are dry trees; if he suffer, why may not they expect to suffer? And what then shall the damnation of sinners be! Even the sufferings of Christ preach terror to obstinate transgressors.
23.m:
Here we see Jesus, the Lamb of God, being led to his death. Many people insulted him, but some felt sorry for him. His death was actually a victory over his enemies and brought salvation for us. So instead of crying for him, we should cry about our own sins and those of our children, which led to his death. We should also worry about the punishment we might face if we ignore his love and refuse his help.

If God allowed Jesus to suffer so much as a sacrifice for our sins, imagine what he will do to those who continue to live sinful lives. Our greatest saints seem small compared to Jesus—if he had to suffer, why shouldn’t others expect to suffer too? What will happen to those who are condemned?

The suffering of Jesus shows us how serious God’s justice is. It should scare us into changing our ways.

23.o: As soon as Christ was fastened to the cross, he prayed for those who crucified him. The great thing he died to purchase and procure for us, is the forgiveness of sin. This he prays for. Jesus was crucified between two thieves; in them were shown the different effects the cross of Christ would have upon the children of men in the preaching the gospel. One malefactor was hardened to the last. No troubles of themselves will change a wicked heart. The other was softened at the last: he was snatched as a brand out of the burning, and made a monument of Divine mercy. This gives no encouragement to any to put off repentance to their death-beds, or to hope that they shall then find mercy. It is certain that true repentance is never too late; but it is as certain that late repentance is seldom true. None can be sure they shall have time to repent at death, but every man may be sure he cannot have the advantages this penitent thief had. We shall see the case to be singular, if we observe the uncommon effects of God's grace upon this man. He reproved the other for railing on Christ. He owned that he deserved what was done to him. He believed Jesus to have suffered wrongfully. Observe his faith in this prayer. Christ was in the depth of disgrace, suffering as a deceiver, and not delivered by his Father. He made this profession before the wonders were displayed which put honour on Christ's sufferings, and startled the centurion. He believed in a life to come, and desired to be happy in that life; not like the other thief, to be only saved from the cross. Observe his humility in this prayer. All his request is, Lord, remember me; quite referring it to Jesus in what way to remember him. Thus he was humbled in true repentance, and he brought forth all the fruits for repentance his circumstances would admit. Christ upon the cross, is gracious like Christ upon the throne. Though he was in the greatest struggle and agony, yet he had pity for a poor penitent. By this act of grace we are to understand that Jesus Christ died to open the kingdom of heaven to all penitent, obedient believers. It is a single instance in Scripture; it should teach us to despair of none, and that none should despair of themselves; but lest it should be abused, it is contrasted with the awful state of the other thief, who died hardened in unbelief, though a crucified Saviour was so near him. Be sure that in general men die as they live.
23.m:
When Jesus was nailed to the cross, he asked God to forgive the people who were hurting him. Forgiving sins is what Jesus came to do for us.

Jesus was put on the cross between two criminals. One criminal kept insulting Jesus until the end. Troubles alone won't change a bad heart. But the other criminal changed his ways right before dying. He showed true remorse, just like someone rescued from a fire. 

This doesn’t mean you should wait until the last minute to ask for forgiveness. True repentance is always good, but it’s rare to truly turn around so close to death. Everyone has chances better than this thief did. 

Notice how this thief spoke up against the other one who was being mean to Jesus. He admitted he deserved what was happening to him. And he believed Jesus didn’t deserve what was happening. 

The thief trusted Jesus even though Jesus looked weak and defeated. He asked to be remembered by Jesus, showing real faith. He wanted to go to heaven, unlike the other thief who just wanted to avoid pain here.

He asked Jesus to remember him kindly, leaving everything else up to Jesus. This shows true humility and repentance. Even while suffering greatly, Jesus still cared about this man. 

From this, we learn that Jesus died to give eternal life to anyone who truly repents and follows him.

This is just one example in the Bible. It teaches us not to give up on anyone, including ourselves. But we shouldn’t misuse it either. The other thief died without faith, even though Jesus was right there being crucified. People usually die the way they lived.

23.o: We have here the death of Christ magnified by the wonders that attended it, and his death explained by the words with which he breathed out his soul. He was willing to offer himself. Let us seek to glorify God by true repentance and conversion; by protesting against those who crucify the Saviour; by a sober, righteous, and godly life; and by employing our talents in the service of Him who died for us and rose again.
23.m:
Here we see the death of Christ made special by the amazing things that happened around it. His death is explained through the words he spoke as he gave up his life. 

Christ willingly offered himself. We should try to honor God by truly turning away from sin and changing our lives. We should speak out against those who harm the Savior. We should live a humble, honest, and godly life. And we should use our abilities to serve him, who died for us and then came back to life.

23.o: Many, though they do not make any show in outward profession, yet, like Joseph of Arimathea, will be far more ready to do real service, when there is occasion, than others who make a greater noise. Christ was buried in haste, because the sabbath drew on. Weeping must not hinder sowing. Though they were in tears for the death of their Lord, yet they must prepare to keep holy the sabbath. When the sabbath draws on, there must be preparation. Our worldly affairs must be so ordered, that they may not hinder us from our sabbath work; and our holy affections so stirred up, that they may carry us on in it. In whatever business we engage, or however our hearts may be affected, let us never fail to get ready for, and to keep holy, the day of sacred rest, which is the Lord's day.
23.m:
Some people help quietly without making a big fuss, just like Joseph of Arimathea did. They’re quicker to serve than those who talk a lot about faith.

Jesus was buried quickly because the Sabbath was coming soon. Even though they were sad over Jesus’ death, they still needed to get ready for the Sabbath.

When the Sabbath approaches, we need to be prepared. We should organize our daily tasks so they don’t stop us from keeping the Sabbath holy. And we should have a good attitude toward worship.

No matter what else is going on, we should always be ready to honor God’s special day of rest.

24.o: See the affection and respect the women showed to Christ, after he was dead and buried. Observe their surprise when they found the stone rolled away, and the grave empty. Christians often perplex themselves about that with which they should comfort and encourage themselves. They look rather to find their Master in his grave-clothes, than angels in their shining garments. The angels assure them that he is risen from the dead; is risen by his own power. These angels from heaven bring not any new gospel, but remind the women of Christ's words, and teach them how to apply them. We may wonder that these disciples, who believed Jesus to be the Son of God and the true Messiah, who had been so often told that he must die, and rise again, and then enter into his glory, who had seen him more than once raise the dead, yet should be so backward to believe his raising himself. But all our mistakes in religion spring from ignorance or forgetfulness of the words Christ has spoken. Peter now ran to the sepulchre, who so lately ran from his Master. He was amazed. There are many things puzzling and perplexing to us, which would be plain and profitable, if we rightly understood the words of Christ.
24.m:
Notice how much the women loved Jesus even after he died and was buried. They were surprised to see the stone moved and the tomb empty. Sometimes people get confused instead of feeling happy when they hear good news. 

The women looked for Jesus in his burial clothes instead of seeing the angels' bright robes. The angels told them Jesus had risen from the dead. He rose up on his own power. 

These angels didn't bring any new message. Instead, they reminded the women of what Jesus said before. This helped the women understand better.

It’s surprising that some followers doubted Jesus even though they knew he was the Son of God and promised to come back. They heard Jesus talk about rising from the dead many times. Yet, they still struggled to believe it.

Peter rushed to check out the tomb, just like he fled earlier. He was shocked. Many confusing things become clear when we remember what Jesus taught.

24.o: This appearance of Jesus to the two disciples going to Emmaus, happened the same day that he rose from the dead. It well becomes the disciples of Christ to talk together of his death and resurrection; thus they may improve one another's knowledge, refresh one another's memory, and stir up each other's devout affections. And where but two together are well employed in work of that kind, he will come to them, and make a third. Those who seek Christ, shall find him: he will manifest himself to those that inquire after him; and give knowledge to those who use the helps for knowledge which they have. No matter how it was, but so it was, they did not know him; he so ordering it, that they might the more freely discourse with him. Christ's disciples are often sad and sorrowful, even when they have reason to rejoice; but through the weakness of their faith, they cannot take the comfort offered to them. Though Christ is entered into his state of exaltation, yet he notices the sorrows of his disciples, and is afflicted in their afflictions. Those are strangers in Jerusalem, that know not of the death and sufferings of Jesus. Those who have the knowledge of Christ crucified, should seek to spread that knowledge. Our Lord Jesus reproved them for the weakness of their faith in the Scriptures of the Old Testament. Did we know more of the Divine counsels as far as they are made known in the Scriptures, we should not be subject to the perplexities we often entangle ourselves in. He shows them that the sufferings of Christ were really the appointed way to his glory; but the cross of Christ was that to which they could not reconcile themselves. Beginning at Moses, the first inspired writer of the Old Testament, Jesus expounded to them the things concerning himself. There are many passages throughout all the Scriptures concerning Christ, which it is of great advantage to put together. We cannot go far in any part, but we meet with something that has reference to Christ, some prophecy, some promise, some prayer, some type or other. A golden thread of gospel grace runs through the whole web of the Old Testament. Christ is the best expositor of Scripture; and even after his resurrection, he led people to know the mystery concerning himself, not by advancing new notions, but by showing how the Scripture was fulfilled, and turning them to the earnest study of it.
24.m:
This meeting between Jesus and the two disciples on the road to Emmaus happened on the very day he came back to life. It’s good for followers of Christ to talk about his death and resurrection. They help each other learn more, remember better, and feel closer to God. When just two people discuss such topics, Jesus will join them too. People who look for Christ will find him. He reveals himself to those who search for him and teaches those who use what they already know. 

The disciples didn’t recognize Jesus right away because God wanted them to speak freely with him. Sometimes, even when there’s reason to be happy, Christ’s followers feel sad due to weak faith. Even though Jesus now enjoys high honor, he cares deeply about his followers’ troubles. Strangers in Jerusalem don’t know about Jesus' death and suffering. Those who know about Christ should tell others.

Jesus corrected them for not understanding the Old Testament scriptures correctly. Knowing God’s plans revealed in the Bible would prevent us from getting confused. He explained that his suffering led to his glory, even if they couldn’t see it then. Starting with Moses, the first writer of the Old Testament, Jesus taught them about himself. The entire Bible contains verses about Christ, prophecies, promises, prayers, and types. Gospel truths run like gold threads through the whole book of the Old Testament.

Christ is the best teacher of the Bible. Even after he rose from the dead, he helped people understand the mysteries about himself by showing how the Scriptures were fulfilled. He did this by guiding them to study the Bible carefully.

24.o: If we would have Christ dwell with us, we must be earnest with him. Those that have experienced the pleasure and profit of communion with him, cannot but desire more of his company. He took bread, and blessed it, and brake, and gave to them. This he did with his usual authority and affection, with the same manner, perhaps with the same words. He here teaches us to crave a blessing on every meal. See how Christ by his Spirit and grace makes himself known to the souls of his people. He opens the Scriptures to them. He meets them at his table, in the ordinance of the Lord's supper; is known to them in breaking of bread. But the work is completed by the opening of the eyes of their mind; yet it is but short views we have of Christ in this world, but when we enter heaven, we shall see him for ever. They had found the preaching powerful, even when they knew not the preacher. Those Scriptures which speak of Christ, will warm the hearts of his true disciples. That is likely to do most good, which affects us with the love of Jesus in dying for us. It is the duty of those to whom he has shown himself, to let others know what he has done for their souls. It is of great use for the disciples of Christ to compare their experiences, and tell them to each other.
24.m:
To have Christ stay with us, we need to ask him to do so. People who have enjoyed talking with him want more time with him. 

He took some bread, said a prayer over it, broke it, and shared it with them. He did all this in his usual way, showing both power and care. We should pray before eating too. 

Christ shows himself to his followers through the Holy Spirit. He helps them understand the Bible. At his table, during Communion, he lets them recognize him. Yet, our understanding is still limited. In heaven, we'll see him clearly forever.

They were moved by his teachings without knowing who he was. The parts of the Bible about Jesus touch believers deeply. What moves us most is seeing how much Jesus loved us by dying for us.

Those who know Christ should share what he’s done for them. It’s helpful for Christians to talk about their experiences with God.

24.o: Jesus appeared in a miraculous manner, assuring the disciples of his peace, though they had so lately forsaken him, and promising spiritual peace with every blessing. Many troublesome thoughts which disquiet our minds, rise from mistakes concerning Christ. All the troublesome thoughts which rise in our hearts at any time, are known to the Lord Jesus, and are displeasing to him. He spake with them on their unreasonable unbelief. Nothing had passed but what was foretold by the prophets, and necessary for the salvation of sinners. And now all men should be taught the nature and necessity of repentance, in order to the forgiveness of their sins. And these blessings were to be sought for, by faith in the name of Jesus. Christ by his Spirit works on the minds of men. Even good men need to have their understandings opened. But that we may have right thoughts of Christ, there needs no more than to be made to understand the Scriptures.
24.m:
Jesus showed up in an amazing way to assure his followers he was peaceful, even though they had recently left him. He promised them spiritual peace and every kind of blessing.

Many worries that trouble us come from misunderstandings about who Jesus is. The Lord Jesus knows all the worries that come up in our hearts and finds them upsetting.

He talked to them about their unfair doubts. Everything that happened was predicted by the prophets and needed for saving people from their sins.

Now everyone should learn about the importance of being sorry for their wrongs to get their sins forgiven. These blessings could be found through believing in Jesus' name.

Christ uses his Spirit to work on people’s minds. Good people still need help understanding things clearly. To think rightly about Jesus, we just need to understand the Scriptures better.

24.o: Christ ascended from Bethany, near the Mount of Olives. There was the garden in which his sufferings began; there he was in his agony. Those that would go to heaven, must ascend thither from the house of sufferings and sorrows. The disciples did not see him rise out of the grave; his resurrection could be proved by their seeing him alive afterwards: but they saw him ascend into heaven; they could not otherwise have a proof of his ascension. He lifted up his hands, and blessed them. He did not go away in displeasure, but in love, he left a blessing behind him. As he arose, so he ascended, by his own power. They worshipped him. This fresh display of Christ's glory drew from them fresh acknowledgments. They returned to Jerusalem with great joy. The glory of Christ is the joy of all true believers, even while they are here in this world. While waiting for God's promises, we must go forth to meet them with our praises. And nothing better prepares the mind for receiving the Holy Ghost. Fears are silenced, sorrows sweetened and allayed, and hopes kept up. And this is the ground of a Christian's boldness at the throne of grace; yea, the Father's throne is the throne of grace to us, because it is also the throne of our Mediator, Jesus Christ. Let us rely on his promises, and plead them. Let us attend his ordinances, praise and bless God for his mercies, set our affections on things above, and expect the Redeemer's return to complete our happiness. Amen. Even so, Lord Jesus, come quickly.
24.m:
Jesus went up to heaven from Bethany, close to the Mount of Olives. That’s where his suffering started and where he felt very sad. People who want to go to heaven need to leave from places filled with pain and trouble. 

The disciples didn’t see him come back to life after dying, but they knew he was alive again because they saw him later. But they did see him go up to heaven. Seeing him go up showed they were right about what happened.

He raised his hands and said a blessing over them. He wasn’t angry when he left, but loving. When he rose from the dead, he used his own power. The disciples worshiped him. Seeing his glory made them happy and thankful.

They went back to Jerusalem full of joy. Knowing about Jesus’ glory makes true believers joyful even now. We should wait for God’s promises by praising him. Praise helps quiet fears, ease sadness, and keep hope strong. It gives Christians courage to pray boldly. The Father’s throne is a place of mercy for us because Jesus sits there too. We can trust his promises and ask for help through prayer.

Let’s follow Jesus’ teachings, thank God for his kindness, look forward to being with him, and wait for him to come back. Come soon, Lord Jesus.
